course_title: Introduction to Role of Science and Technology Education in Society
course_description:
This course provides an introduction to science and technology education in primary schooling. The course reviews the appropriate scientific ideas and practices for these levels of schooling. It builds understanding about how scientific knowledge can be used in current society ranging from personal health and well being to global concerns such as climate change and sustainability. The close link between science and technology forms an underlying theme in the course. The teaching strategies used in the course reflect international best practice and the Australian Curriculum.

The ideas and understandings in this course are the basis for a later course in science teaching pedagogy (EDUC3730). Effective study should ensure you retain these ideas and understandings when you need them to complete this course. You will also need these ideas and understandings when teaching science as a primary school teacher. This course introduces science and technologies topics, elicits your understanding, develops deeper conceptual understandings, and helps you articulate your understanding.ﾠ

restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BEd(Primary)

course_aims: This course provides an introduction to science and technology education in primary schools. The course reviews the appropriate scientific and technological ideas and practices for these levels of schooling. It builds understanding about how scientific knowledge can be used in current society ranging from personal health and wellbeing to global concerns such as climate change and sustainability. The close link between science and technology forms an underlying theme in the course. The teaching strategies used in the course reflect international best practice and the Australian Curriculum.

learning_outcomes.description: Develop, use and evaluate scientific explanations about everyday events including those that apply to technological practices. (APST 2.1, 2.2, 2.5)
Select and evaluate science and technology resources for use in the primary years of schooling classrooms. (APST 1.2, 1.5, 3.4)
Examine and compare the processes of science and technology and their use in schools (APST 4.1, 4.4)
Examine science and technology issues and plan for their use in the classroom setting. (APST 2.2, 2.6, 3.4, 5.2)
Evaluate social consequences of scientific and technological knowledge and practice. (APST 2.2, 2.5, 2.6)

assessment_details.title: Student Safety Declaration
assessment_details.task_description: All students are expected to complete a safety declaration in order to work in the laboratory. The university requires this declaration to be current within 5 years. Records are kept on the OHS database. Laboratory conditions for education are considered to be any space where scientific activity is taking place. Students must be aware that this also translates to primary classrooms where safety is paramount and risk assessment is critical. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Topic quizzes in class
assessment_details.task_description: Starting in week 4, a written timed quiz will be administered in the lecture each fortnight. The quiz must be completed in person on the sheet provided. Responses must be handwritten unless there is an accessibility requirement. You are responsible for bringing your own pen or pencil. Students are not permitted to access electronic devices of any kind during the writing task, but may consult printed or handwritten notes. Any attempt to use Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. There are a total of 5 quizzes, each worth 6% of the overall course grade, with a focus on each of the content modules in the course. It will be held the week following the completion of the module. The quizzes may include short-answer questions and/or multiple-choice questions that refer to content from the readings, lectures, and/or tutorials. Multiple-choice questions will be marked as correct or incorrect. For each short-answer question, you will receive 50% for a partially correct answer, and zero per cent for responses that are incorrect or incomplete (whether unsubmitted because of non-attendance, submitted but blank, submitted but not responding to the question, illegible, etc.). You will also receive zero per cent if you have not followed an instruction to put away your device. Feedback and answers will be given in the class immediately after the quiz. A score out of 6 for each quiz will be recorded on Gradebook on the Blackboard in a week. Criteria & Marking: The overall grade for this task is calculated as a percentage by taking the points scored across all five quizzes and comparing it to the total points possible, e.g., 24 points allocated for correct answers out of 30 total points: 24/30 x 100 = 80% This percentage is mapped to the UQ grading schema (below) to display a grade: 84.5% and 100% = 7 74.5% and less than 84.5% = 6 64.5% and less than 74.5% = 5 49.5% and less than 64.5% = 4 46.5% and less than 49.5% = 3 23.5% and less than 46.5% = 2 0 and less than 23.5% = 1 Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission in the lecture for each instance:
Biological Sciences in Week 4 Physical Sciences in Week 6 Technologies in Week 8 Chemical Sciences in Week 10 Earth and Space Sciences in Week 12
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Please note: This is an in-class assessment task, and students are NOT able to apply for an extension via myRequests at myUQ. Please contact the Course Coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available. If there is a genuine reason why you cannot attend the class, a make-up test may be offered.
Late submission
Late submission is not possible for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Product + Explanation
assessment_details.task_description: Rube Goldberg was famous for drawing cartoons that depicted very complicated machines designed to perform a simple task. The actual construction of a Rube Goldberg machine requires a good understanding of energy transfers, forces, and simple machines. You will design and construct a Rube Goldberg machine that utilises at least three (3) different simple machines and at least two (2) energy transfers to successfully complete the end task. Machine Rules The machine must complete a described task (as reliably as possible). Your machine must have a title. The machine must contain at least three simple machines and 2 energy transfers There must be a minimum of 5 steps (individual) or 8 steps (pair). There is no maximum number of steps. No live animals or hazardous materials may be used. The machine must not imply or contain anything inappropriate. Any loose or flying objects must remain within the set boundaries of the machine. Task products 1. The Machine – individually or in a pair You will present your machine in a video. The video should record your machine in action with no “cuts” and editing, and comment on the steps in the machine. You should appear in your video with your machine. 2. Individual task – each student will also complete a written assignment as a conclusion to the project. This will include: The goal (what were you trying to accomplish?) A detailed drawing of your machine, labelling each step with A, B, C … Provide a written explanation of each step Include a description of the energy transfers A completed Risk assessment form (a template has been provided) Reflection documenting: the process of developing and executing the task issues and challenges associated with demonstrating a concept in physical form links to the Australian Curriculum: Science. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assessment is submitted through Turnitin.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Reflective Journal
assessment_details.task_description: You are to create a reflective journal that demonstrates how your understanding of science and technology content develops across the semester. Your reflections should show how your thinking evolves as you engage with the course concepts, readings, practical activities, and professional experience. This journal is intended to help you build a deep, connected understanding of the science and technologies learning areas you will be expected to teach. Task Product: Reflective Journal: Using the submission template provided, you are required to respond to a series of reflective questions. These questions will focus on: science and technologies content, skills, and pedagogy placement observations of teaching science and/or technologies in the classroom Your response to each question should be approximately 200 words. Each response should draw on: science and technologies concepts theories and research teaching strategies course learning activities your own observations, experiences, and collaborations. Your reflections should be critical, not just descriptive, and show how your understanding is evolving. Although you are encouraged to complete the reflections progressively throughout the semester, you will submit the full set of responses together on the final due date. learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction Introduction to course content and science and technologies education, including teaching primary science, scientific literacy, science inquiry and science pedagogy. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 3 (02 Mar - 15 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Biological Sciences As we observe and learn, it is in our nature to try to organise or make sense of these observations. Our brain acts like a filing system, and we use this system to make connections that build our understanding. How can living things be organised? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 5 (16 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Physical Sciences Forces influence objects that are at rest or already in motion. Understanding Newton's three laws provide the basis for recognising how we can manipulate force and effort to our advantage. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 7 (30 Mar - 19 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Technologies Technologies permeate much of what we do every day in and out of the classroom. How do we teach content about which our students already have such proficiency? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Week 9 (20 Apr - 03 May)
learning_activities.topic: Chemical Sciences A limited number of different elements make up all matter on Earth. The different combinations of these elements allow for an increasing number of materials that may occur naturally and be used for specific purposes or may be man-made to improve properties. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 10 To Week 11 (04 May - 17 May)
learning_activities.topic: Earth & Space Sciences Humans have looked to the sky for both practical purposes as well as creative interpretation. The explanations regarding the relationship between the Sun, Moon and Earth have developed from speculation to fact over a long period of time. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Science pedagogy A taster of science pedagogical approaches in preparation for your placement and the next science course in your program. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Break for Professional Experience placement No classes scheduled while undertaking professional experience (placement) in an accompanying course.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Occupational Health and Safety in the Laboratory Guideline Learning activities
